name: Korean Fried Chicken (Yangnyeom-Chikin)
why: Double-fried to shatteringly crispy perfection and glazed in a sweet-spicy gochujang sauce — Korean fried chicken is one of the most satisfying techniques to master. Perfect for a weekend project that delivers restaurant-quality results at home.
author: Henry
ingredients: 2 lbs (900g) skin-on, deboned chicken thighs or wings/drumsticks
¾ cup water (add more if needed)
Neutral cooking oil (vegetable oil preferred), for deep frying

steps: Prep the chicken: debone chicken thighs (cut into 1½–2 inch pieces with skin on) or cut wings in half so wing and drumstick are separate pieces.
In a bowl, combine salt, pepper, garlic powder, ginger powder, and onion powder. Whisk thoroughly to mix dry ingredients.
Add water and vinegar to the bowl. Whisk until combined — the marinade should be thick, similar to applesauce. Add water one tablespoon at a time if needed.
Place chicken in an airtight container, pour the wet marinade over top, and make sure every piece is thoroughly coated.
Refrigerate for at least 12 hours (up to 18 hours) for maximum flavor penetration.
Remove chicken from the fridge about 30 minutes before frying to come to room temperature — frying cold chicken risks raw centers.
In a shallow bowl, combine potato starch, all-purpose flour, and salt for the dry coating.
Take each piece of chicken, shake off excess marinade, then coat thoroughly in the dry batter. Pat off any excess powder and place on a wire rack.
Heat about 2 inches of vegetable oil in a heavy-bottomed pot to 375°F (190°C).
Using tongs, carefully lower chicken pieces into the oil — do not crowd the pot. Fry in batches.
Fry each batch for 2–4 minutes depending on size, turning occasionally so both sides brown evenly. The outside should be light golden.
Use a thermometer to verify chicken reaches 165°F (74°C) internally. Remove and place on a paper-towel-lined wire rack.
Remove any charred bits from the bottom of the pot with a small strainer.
Double-fry: return chicken to the oil in small batches and cook for another 2–3 minutes until the outer layer is very crispy and medium to dark golden brown.
Remove chicken and drain on paper towels over wire racks.
In a saucepan over medium heat, add sesame oil and minced garlic. Cook until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
Lower heat to medium-low. Add gochujang, honey, ketchup, and soy sauce.
Stir and simmer gently for about 1 minute — warm the sauce through but do not boil.
In a large mixing bowl, add the fried chicken and pour about half the sauce over top.
Toss vigorously until every piece is evenly coated. Add more sauce if needed.
Serve immediately with daikon radish pickles, french fries, or rice. Pairs perfectly with a cold beer.

pro_tips: The double-fry technique is the secret: the first fry cooks the chicken through, the second fry at higher heat creates the shatteringly crispy crust that stays crisp even after glazing.
Potato starch is non-negotiable — it creates a lighter, crispier crust than flour alone. Do not substitute with cornstarch if you can help it.
Ginger powder in the wet marinade is a Korean trick that adds warmth and depth you won't get from fresh ginger alone.
